Summary: NVIDIA hardware OpenGL can crash MATLAB when you are using large data sets with 3-D graphing functions.
Description:
Using a 3-D graphing function such as  mesh  or surf  causes the NVIDIA opengl driver to crash along with MATLAB. The following is the function stack that the crash log provides: Stack Trace (from fault):[ 0] 0x0000000016627c30 C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12745776 DrvPresentBuffers+02727712[ 1] 0x00000000165f107e C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12521598 DrvPresentBuffers+02503534[ 2] 0x0000000016581025 C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12062757 DrvPresentBuffers+02044693[ 3] 0x00000000165872aa C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12087978 DrvPresentBuffers+02069914[ 4] 0x0000000016587464 C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12088420 DrvPresentBuffers+02070356[ 5] 0x000000001658ba29 C:\Windows\system32
voglv64.DLL+12106281 DrvPresentBuffers+02088217[ 6] 0x0000000079e94177 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2013a in\win64\glren.dll+00082295 GLRenderEngineRegister+00019159[ 7] 0x0000000079e94749 C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2013a in\win64\glren.dll+00083785 GLRenderEngineRegister+00020649
Workaround: 1. Use software opengl to render any 3D graphical plots of large data sets as follows:opengl software2. Use painters renderer for plotting large data sets. This option can be slower depending on the size of the data.set(gcf, 'Renderer', 'painters');

Summary: Closing figure can cause segmentation violation
Description: A MATLAB segmentation violation can occur when some MATLAB code tries to access a figure that the user has closed. When this happens, a stack trace is generated with this text near the top of the stack. pmoReleasePointer Users have experienced this behavior under the following circumstances: Closing a figure that was used as an input for uiwait  Closing a figure created by implay  Closing dialog figures, such as those created by msgbox  Closing a figure that a program is interacting with Closing a figure before MATLAB has enough time to finish creating it  ?

Summary: Blurry desktop on Retina displays on a Mac
Description: Due to a bug in the version of Java used by MATLAB in R2013b and R2014a, the desktop and many windows in MATLAB and toolboxes display slightly blurry text on MacBook Retina displays.
Workaround: Here are some options for improving the visual appearance of MATLAB: Open in Low Resolution: This option is the simplest to execute, but it only provides a slight improvement. After completion of these steps, the MATLAB Desktop will appear pixelated, but not blurry.To use this option: 1.  Open a new "Finder" window and navigate to the "Applications" directory.2.  Select the MATLAB icon and choose "File" > "Get Info".3.  Check the option "Open in Low Resolution".4.  Launch MATLAB.   Change the Display Settings: This option is also simple to execute, but it changes your Mac's display settings to shrink all content and give the appearance of more space. Because the application is smaller, the blurriness (or pixelation, if combined with the "Open in Low Resolution" option) is less noticeable.To use this option: 1.  Choose "System Preferences" from the Apple menu in the top-left corner of your screen.2.  Choose "Display" to open the display settings.3.  In the "Display" tab, ensure that the "Resolution" type is set to "Scaled".4.  Select the option for "More Space". This will decrease the size of the content on your screen and offer more space.5.  Launch MATLAB.

Summary: Workspace browser can slow down Simulink? simulation performance when there are a large number of variables in the workspace
Description: Simulink? simulations initiated from a menu rather than by using the sim  command can run significantly more slowly when the Workspace browser is open and when there are a thousands of variables or more in the current MATLAB? workspace.
Workaround: Close the Workspace browser and the Variables editor before running the simulation. Alternatively, run the simulation using the sim command.

Summary: On non-English platforms, MATLAB Report Generator may garble certain character sequences such as "~((" when generating Word/Rich Text Format (RTF) documents via DSSSL stylesheets
Description: On non-English platforms, MATLAB Report Generator may garble certain character sequences such as "~((" when generating Word/Rich Text Format (RTF) documents via DSSSL stylesheets
Workaround: When generating Word documents, use the Word (from template) file format (introduced in R2014b) .The older file format Word Document (RTF) will continue to exhibit this issue.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Exceeding 64 CAN transmit or receive connections results in error
Description: When you have more than 64 CAN transmit or receive, or 32 virtual connections to a MATLAB session or a Simulink model, you may get an error when you try to establish an additional connection. When you try to generate code in Simulink normal mode, you might see one of the following errors. In release R2013a and later: Unable to query hardware information for the selected CAN channel object.   In release R2012b: boost thread resource allocation error.   In release R2012a and earlier: An unhandled error occurred with CAN device.
Workaround: This is a vendor imposed limitation. Vector limits CAN transmit or receive channels to 64 physical and 32 virtual channels.To work around this issue, you can use a Mux block in Simulink to manage your channels or reuse channels in MATLAB without creating new ones.

Summary: MATLAB Builder EX and MATLAB Builder NE COM events are only passed to the thread that creates the COM object.
Description: When using the %#event  pragma, Builder Ex and Builder NE COM events are only passed to the thread that creates the COM object. If you are using the %#event  pragma in your MATLAB code, you should call the COM component from the same thread in which you created it; otherwise, indeterminate behavior results.
Workaround: If you are using the %#event pragma in your MATLAB code, you should call the COM component from the same thread in which you created it; otherwise, indeterminate behavior results.

Summary: On Macintosh systems, zooming does not change equation size in help
Description: On Macintosh systems, zooming in or out in the Help browser or Web browser help pages changes the font size, but does not change the size of equations.
Workaround: For MATLAB? R2014a, download the patch in Bug Report 870843

Summary: Unexpected legend strings appear after plotting multiple series via a single plot command.
Description: When multiple data series are plotted via a single plot command that includes a single DisplayName  property/value pair, the resulting DisplayName  for the plotted objects contain unexpected values The following example illustrates this behavior. h = plot(rand(3),'DisplayName','name'); legend show; get(h,'DisplayName') ans =  'getcolumn(name,1)' 'getcolumn(name,2)' 'getcolumn(name,3)'
Workaround: Pass the desired strings directly to the legend commandh=plot(rand(3));legend name name nameget(h,'DisplayName')ans = 'name' 'name' 'name'

Summary: MATLAB? unresponsive when using doc or web on some Macintosh systems.
Description: On some Macintosh systems, when the "Enable Access for Assistive Devices" setting is on, attempting to use the Help browser or Web browser may cause MATLAB to become unresponsive or terminate abruptly.
Workaround: For MATLAB R2014a on OSX:To install this patch, you need the name and password of an account with Administrator privileges.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report.Look for the file in your Downloads folder, the Desktop, or wherever your Web browser saves downloaded files.If your Web browser did not unzip the patch file for you, double-click the file in the Finder to unzip it.Double-click the .dmg file to mount the disk image.At the top level of the mounted disk image is an application such as R2014a_patch_870843.app. Double-click to launch it.Select your MATLAB installation. Select the root folder of the install, for example, MATLAB_R2014a.app.Enter the name and password of an account with Administrator privileges.The patch, containing an upgrade to MATLAB's HTML rendering libraries, will be installed in your MATLAB installation.When the upgrade is complete, you can launch MATLAB.After launching MATLAB, reset the HTML rendering preference by running:>> webutils.htmlrenderer('default')For MATLAB versions prior to R2014a:If you are using OSX 10.9 (Maverick), before starting MATLAB, follow these steps:1. From the Apple menu, select System Preferences.2. Open Security & Privacy.3. Click the Privacy tab.4. Select Accessibility in the list on the left.5. Uncheck any items listed under Allow the apps below to control your computer.If you are using a version of OSX prior to 10.9, before starting MATLAB, follow these steps:1. From the Apple menu, select System Preferences.2. In the search box, enter "enable assistive devices".3. Click on the result to display the option.4. Clear the option for Enable access for assistive devices.If you do not want to disable this setting, use the following instructions.R2013a and R2013b:At the MATLAB command prompt, type the following, and then restart MATLAB.>> com.mathworks.mlwidgets.html.HtmlComponentFactory.setBrowserProperty('JxBrowser.BrowserType','Mozilla15'); MATLAB retains this setting across sessions.To set the browser back to its default value, type the following at the MATLAB command prompt:>> com.mathworks.mlwidgets.html.HtmlComponentFactory.setBrowserProperty('JxBrowser.BrowserType','Safari');If this workaround does not resolve the problem, use the following R2012b workaround.R2012b:At the MATLAB command prompt, type the following, and then restart MATLAB. (Be aware that issuing this command results in a degraded appearance of documentation pages and loss of some functionality.)>> com.mathworks.mlwidgets.html.HtmlComponentFactory.setDefaultType('HTMLRENDERER');MATLAB retains this setting across sessions.To set the browser back to its default value, type the following at the MATLAB command prompt:>> com.mathworks.mlwidgets.html.HtmlComponentFactory.setDefaultType(' ');

Summary: Graphics incorrectly drawn when panning fitted surface
Description: With certain Intel? HD integrated graphics cards, when you pan a surface in the Curve Fitting Tool, the surface is drawn incorrectly. During the pan, the faces of the surface are drawn in the wrong places while the edges of the mesh remain in the correct place. The problem is caused by a bug in the driver for Intel? HD integrated graphics cards. This is a cosmetic graphics problem that does not affect your surface fit model. The surface is correctly drawn when you stop panning.
Workaround: Upgrading the driver on the graphics card might resolve the issue.

Summary: Setting the FontSize property for text objects in a figure on Linux does not change the font size.
Description: Setting the FontSize property for text objects in a figure on Linux does not change the font size. For example: t = 0:0.1:10; y = sin(t); figure('renderer','zbuffer') plot(t,y) t1 = text(4,sin(4),'This is the point when t = 4','FontSize',50); t2 = title('Test Graph','FontSize',10); ?

Summary: On Mac OS X 10.7.2 and above, executing EDIT in '-nodisplay' mode can cause an X11 crash or a warning dialog
Description: X11 2.6.3, which is part of Apple's OS X 10.7.2 update that was released in October 2011, is incompatible with the libfreetype.6.dylib  library shipped with MATLAB. This can lead to a crash or a warning dialog when executing EDIT in '-nodisplay' mode.
Workaround: Create a link in the MATLAB bin/maci64 directory to the new version of libfreetype.6.dylib shipped by Apple. Launch MATLAB and execute the following commands in the Command Window:cd (matlabroot)cd bin/maci64!mv libfreetype.6.dylib libfreetype.6.dylib.save!ln -s /usr/X11/lib/libfreetype.6.dylib libfreetype.6.dylibThen relaunch MATLAB.Note that if you are using a 32-bit version of MATLAB, substitute bin/maci for bin/maci64 in the command above.

Summary: Setting?the ADCTimingMode property might fail with a?warning
Description: For National Instruments devices that support the ADCTimingMode  property, adding additional input channels after setting the ADCTimingMode ?to 'HighSpeed'  results in a warning and fails to set the ADCTimingMode  to 'HighSpeed'.  For example, using the NI-9219 cDAQ module:   s = daq.createSession('ni');  deviceID = 'cDAQ1Mod2';  addAnalogInputChannel(s,deviceID,0,'Voltage');  s.Channels(1).ADCTimingMode = 'HighSpeed';  addAnalogInputChannel(s,deviceID,1,'Voltage');  Warning: This property must be the same for all channels on this device.  All channels associated with this device were updated.   NI Error -200106:   Property must have the same value for all channels on this device.  Property: DAQmx_AI_ADCTimingMode  Corresponding Value: DAQmx_Val_HighSpeed
Workaround: Add all input channels to the session and then set the ADCTimingMode of?each channel in the session?to 'HighSpeed'. For example:  s = daq.createSession('ni');  deviceID = 'cDAQ1Mod2';  addAnalogInputChannel(s,deviceID,0,'Voltage');  addAnalogInputChannel(s,deviceID,1,'Voltage');   s.Channels(1).ADCTimingMode = 'HighSpeed';  s.Channels(2).ADCTimingMode = 'HighSpeed';

Summary: Incorrect state logging warnings for models referenced in Accelerator mode
Description: State logging is not supported for states of a fixed-point type that is not built-in. Simulink issues a warning when a state with this type is logged.  If such a state is in a model that is referenced in Accelerator mode, Simulink issue the state logging warning every time the referenced model is recompiled, even if state logging is turned off. State logging is controlled by the parameter States  on the Data Import/Export  page of the Configuration Parameters dialog.

Summary: The output of contourf is incorrect for data which contains -inf.
Description: If you create a filled contour plot of data which contains the value -inf, and specify contour levels, then plot may fill areas which should be left empty.Consider this example:data = peaks; %% Correct plot figure(1) contourf(data,0:1:8) colorbar %% Incorrect plot figure(2) data(1) = -Inf; contourf(data,0:1:8) colorbar
Workaround: It does work if you use an extremely small number instead of -inf. This means that you can do something like this:data(data==-inf) = -1e300;

Summary: Markers are rendered as small dots when the MarkerSize is less than a certain value.
Description: The MarkerSize  property of an object is combined with the Root object's ScreenPixelsPerInch  property to determine the resulting marker size in pixels. For example: h = plot(1:10); ms = get(h,'MarkerSize'); spi = get(0,'ScreenPixelsPerInch'); If ms*spi/72  is less than or equal to 6, then markers are drawn as small dots. To prevent markers from being drawn as small dots, set the MarkerSize  property to a value greater than: 6*72/spi For example, if ScreenPixelsPerInch  is equal to 96, then set the MarkerSize  to a value greater than: 6*72/96 ans = 4.5000
Workaround: Increase the MarkerSize property value until it is large enough for the resolution of the particular monitor.To prevent the marker from being drawn as a small dot, set the MarkerSize property to a value greater than:6*72/ScreenPixelsPerInch?

Summary: Pinned annotations move and unpin when a FIG-file is loaded to MATLAB
Description: When you load a FIG-file with pinned annotations to MATLAB, the annotations might move from their original pinned positions and become unpinned.

Summary: PCOLOR with OpenGL renderer does not render transparent face correctly in the presence of NaNs.
Description: Using PCOLOR with NaNs and transparent face, can result in the total transparency of one level of pixels following NaN values in the PCOLOR plot.

Summary: Error while using inputs of datatype "single" for the COLORMAP function.
Description: Using an input argument of data type "single" with the COLORMAP function returns an error.

Summary: nargin gives incorrect results for plot, plot3, semilogx, semilogy, loglog, fill, fill3, line, patch
Description: The functions plot , plot3 , semilogx , semilogy , loglog , fill , fill3 , line , and patch  all take a variable number of input arguments, so nargin  should return -1 when passed a reference to any of these functions. Instead, nargin  returns 1. nargin(@plot)        % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@plot3)       % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@semilogx)    % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@semilogy)    % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@loglog)      % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@fill)        % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@fill3)       % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@line)        % returns 1 but should be -1nargin(@patch)       % returns 1 but should be -1

Summary: LaTeX strings may look poor when figures are published on Macintosh OS X.
Description: Text strings containing LaTeX appear pixelated when published on Macintosh OS X.

Summary: Constant value block input sources can result in incorrect code.
Description: When the code generator reads constant data, the generator can access memory outside of the constant data storage. As a result, the generator may define constant values incorrectly in the generated code. This error is more likely to occur if the embedded target has no built-in 8 or 16-bit integer data types. The integer types are shown in the Configuration Parameters dialog box on the Hardware Implementation  pane. The problem has been observed on a 64-bit Windows host. ?
Workaround: Use 32-bit data types or a data type that is built-in to the hardware for constants or ground values.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Numerical behavior might differ between generated code and model simulation for continuous-time root outputs
Description: Numerical behavior might differ between generated code and model simulation if all of the following conditions exist: In the model, a continuous time signal feeds a root Outport. The model is configured to generate a step function instead of separate output and update functions. That is, In a GRT-based model, you have cleared the Classic call interface  option, or In an ERT-based model, you have cleared the Classic call interface  option and selected the Single output/update function  option.   You have customized a main.c  or interface file (such as rt_main.c ) to read the output (for example, rtY  data structure) after each base-rate model step for the continuous-time root output.  Under these conditions, a numerical discrepancy might occur because of a data logging difference. While data logged during a simulation is a snapshot of the root Outport values at major time steps, output read from a main.c  file after executing the base-rate model step function potentially reflects intervening minor time steps.
Workaround: To eliminate the discrepancy:Place a Zero-Order Hold block before the continuous-time root Outport.For Embedded Coder, an alternative workaround is to clear the Single output/update function option for your model and place your custom logging code between the execution of the output function code and the update function code.

Summary: Data tip does not re-size data window correctly when its DisplayStyle is set to window.
Description: When a data tip has a custom update function, the text is not displayed properly if it contains more than three lines and its DisplayStyle  is set to window .

Summary: Positions of ActiveX components in a GUI shift upward on Windows 7 due to  resizing window
Description: Activex components placed in a figure window are displaced upwards from their intended positions by a few pixels on Windows 7 32-bit platforms. The issue affects GUIs created in GUIDE. To see this issue: Create a new GUIDE GUI Add a panel (in order to better observe the shift) Place any ActiveX control just under the text on the panel Enlarge the GUI document to be about 800-by-800 pixels by dragging the bottom right corner. (Use the position readout on the right side of the GUIDE status bar.) Run the GUI and notice that the ActiveX control has shifted upwards compared to the position in the design window.  ?
Workaround: There are no known workarounds.

Summary: MATLAB on Mac OS X sometimes does not render surfaces when the data is of very small magnitude.
Description: When the surf  or surface  command is passed data of very small magnitude, like eps*peaks , MATLAB sometimes does not render the surface on Mac OS X. The result is an empty axes displayed in the figure. For example, scale the peaks  data by eps  (smallest number): surf(eps*peaks) ?
Workaround: This problem occurs with the OpenGL renderer. Use the zbuffer renderer instead by setting the figure Renderer property.set(gcf,'Renderer','zbuffer');?

Summary: Persistent variables of functions used in the Interpreted MATLAB Function block might not retain correct results in some cases
Description: A persistent variable of a function used in the Interpreted MATLAB Function block might not retain the correct results when the following conditions hold: The Interpreted MATLAB Function block calls a function myfunc  and: The block parameter MATLAB function  is entered as 'myfunc' instead of 'myfunc(u)' There are breakpoints set in the MATLAB function 'myfunc.m'  There is a persistent variable in 'myfunc.m' that is assigned the value of the input to the function 'myfunc'
Workaround: On the Interpreted MATLAB Function block dialog, set MATLAB function to 'myfunc(u)' instead of 'myfunc'.

Summary: hgload can create segmentation violations in hg:_hgStruct2NHandles
Description: Calling hgload  to load a FIG-file can cause MATLAB to unexpectedly exit with a segmentation violation in hg.dll . The stack trace generated begins with [0] [hexadecimal address][1] hg.dll:_hgStruct2NHandles...

Summary: dsp.SignalReader System object no longer interprets row vector input as column input
Description: The dsp.SignalReader  object reads only across columns of the signal you specify in the Signal  property. Previously, the object assumed row vector input should be interpreted as if it were a column of input.
Workaround: Specify the dsp.SignalReader Signal property data vector as a column vector. To transpose row vector input, use ' (or .' for complex data) For example,  [1 2 3]' changes row vector  1 2 3to column vector    1  2  3

Summary: xlabel and title may be clipped with subplot(1,1,1)
Description: When using subplot(1,1,1)  to create a single subplot in a short, wide figure, xlabel and title may be clipped: %%% BEGIN CODE %%%figure('Units','Inches','Position',[1 4 6 2]);subplot(1,1,1)plot(1:3)title('This title is clipped.');xlabel('This label is clipped.');%%% END CODE %%% If we execute this code without the call to subplot(1,1,1) , the xlabel and title are not clipped.

Summary: MATLAB may crash when closing a figure before other programs finish using it
Description: In some circumstances MATLAB may crash when you close a figure window another MATLAB program was still using.  The typical workflow that causes MATLAB to crash is as follows: Call commands such as print , plottools  on a figure window Close the figure window before print , plottools  finish
Workaround: A possible workaround to prevent MATLAB from crashing would be to leave the figure window open until all programs using it are finished.

Summary: Closing figure window while using ginput can cause MATLAB to crash
Description: Closing a figure while using the ginput  function can cause MATLAB to crash
Workaround: To avoid the crash, terminate the execution of the ginput function by pressing the Return key.For MATLAB R2012a and above, you can also install the patch below to prevent MATLAB from crashing. Installation instructions       Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.       Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.       Exit MATLAB.       Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.                     Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file:                                    Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.                     Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>                                          Extract the files from the .zip file.                                                               Windows Systems*:                                                             Open the .zip file.                                  Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.                                                                                                Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):                            cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>                                                 Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.  *Notes for Windows Systems:      You need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.  To save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode.    If you see ¡°Error: Access is denied¡± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you  may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to  WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting  Run as administrator.    From  within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.    Extract  the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Bar plots show extra patch in when webfigure is rendered
Description: When this code, for example, is run in MATLAB, a bar plot is created. f = figure; b = bar(rand(10,5),'stacked');  However, when this code is used with Webfigures, using either MATLAB Builder JA or MATLAB Builder NE, the rendered output on the Web page displays an extra patch on the lower left corner of the Webfigure. ?
Workaround: This behavior is due to a bug in core MATLAB functionality that Webfigures uses.There is no workaround at this time.

Summary: quiver generates incorrect velocity plot with negative scale input
Description: Notice the different results produced by the following code: %%% BEGIN CODE %%%[x,y] = meshgrid(-2:.2:2,-1:.15:1);z = x .* exp(-x.^2 - y.^2);[px,py] = gradient(z,.2,.15);figure; quiver('v6',x,y,px,py,-0.6);figure; quiver(x,y,px,py,-0.6);%%% END CODE %%%
Workaround: Instead of specifying negative scale, use negative velocity with a positive scale%%% BEGIN CODE %%%figure; quiver(x,y,-px,-py,0.6);%%% END CODE %%%

Summary: Generating a lot of output through a Ghostscript driver can result in "out of memory" errors.
Description: When using MATLAB to generate many output files that make use of a Ghostscript driver such as PDF, you might experience an "out of memory" condition. This is due to a bug in the 3rd-party Ghostscript library used by MATLAB. This bug cannot be fixed until third-party product changes are made in Ghostscript.
Workaround: Consider splitting the output generation over several MATLAB sessions, rather than all within one session.

Summary: Rotated text can appear garbled on Mac OS X 10.6 (Snow Leopard) and later versions.
Description: Rotated text, such as y-axis labels, can appear garbled on Mac OS X 10.6 (Snow Leopard) and later versions. Horizontal text is displayed correctly.
Workaround: Use an annotation or text object, with horizontal text, to label the y-axis. For example:plot(1:10);h=annotation('textbox',[.01 .4 .1 .1]);set(h,'String','ylabel');set(h,'EdgeColor','none');

Summary: patch object coloring does not saturate as expected when specifying CLim
Description: patch  object colors do not saturate as expected when setting the CLIM property. The following code reproduces the issue: x = [0 0 200 50 200 50 0];  y = [0 4 4 3 2 0 0];  patch(x,y,x) ;colormap('jet') ;set(gca,'clim',[50 150]);  The above code does not saturate the color for xlim values <50 and >150.
Workaround: To work around this issue, create a custom colormap that includes the required color for the values below min(CLim) and above max(CLim). The following example describes this approach:x = [0 0 200 50 200 50 0]; y = [0 4 4 3 2 0 0]; cmap = jet(100);cmapBeg = repmat(cmap(1 ,:),50,1); % First 50 values are the samecmapEnd = repmat(cmap(end,:),50,1); % Last 50 values are the samecmap = [cmapBeg; cmap; cmapEnd];h=patch(x,y,x);set(gcf,'renderer','zbuffer')colormap(cmap);colorbar

Summary: Setting axes tight with pcolor and a line object makes the pcolor plot disappear
Description: This issue occurs on certain graphics cards when using OpenGL  renderer. The following code reproduces the issue: opengl hardwaref = figure('renderer','OpenGL'); x = -5:5; y = x; [mX mY] = meshgrid(x,y) ; h = pcolor(x,y,mX+mY+10); axis equal tight l = line(1,-1,1, 'color','r', 'marker', 'p');  The issue occurs due to one of the following: pcolor  creates a surface with ZData = 0  Setting axis tight and the line with ZData = 1 , sets the ZLIM  of the axis to [0 1]   On certain graphics cards, the surface (in this case the result of the pcolor  plot) gets clipped and disappears.
Workaround: You can use one of the following techniques to workaround the issue:Change the renderer to use zbuffer or painters. This can be done use the following commands:set(gcf,'Renderer','zbuffer')orset(gcf,'Renderer','painters')Update your graphics driver or use the software implementation of OpenGL. You can enable the software implementation of OpenGL using the following command:opengl('software')If the plot is a 2D plot, omit setting the ZData for any of the objects. For the above example, the following command plots the marker at Z = 0 and you should not see the clipping issue:l = line(1,-1, 'color','r', 'marker', 'p');???

Summary: hgload can create segmentation violations in library calls
Description: Calling hgload  to load a FIG-file can cause MATLAB to unexpectedly exit with a segmentation violation in hg.dll . The stack trace generated begins with hg.dll:void __cdecl figureAllPositionPostSet...

Summary: Plotting markers can affect the line joins in wide lines
Description: Plotting markers when using the painters  renderer (default) can affect the way wide lines are joined. The following code reproduces the issue; the presence of the markers cause subplots 1 and 3 to look different despite the data being the same. data = rand(20,1);figure ax1 = subplot(311); p1 = plot(data,'linewidth',25); ax2 = subplot(312); p2 = plot(data); ax3 = subplot(313); p3 = plot(data,'linewidth',25); set([ax1 ax2 ax3],'yLim',[-0.25 1.25]) set(p2,'marker','s')   This issue does not happen with small line widths or with other renderers.

Summary: Fonts are the wrong size or are clipped inconsistently on MAC 10.5
Description: ON MAC OS X 10.5: When creating  plots with y-labels, or other rotated text, the text clips inconsistently. GUIs created on older versions of MAC OS X appear with text of the wrong size in uicontrols . This problem is caused because MAC OS X 10.5 introduced more accurate calculation of the ScreenPixelsPerInch  root property. Query the current value of the property with this command: get(0,'ScreenPixelsPerInch') Older versions of MAC OS X always used a value of 72.
Workaround: The simplest solution is to change the root property ScreenPixelsPerInch to a hard-coded value that matches previous versions of MACOS.set(0,'ScreenPixelsPerInch',72)A better solution is to create your uicontrols using a value for FontUnits that doesn't depend on ScreenPixelsPerInch, such as pixels:uicontrol('String','Moose','FontUnits','pixels','FontSize',12)Alternatively, create the GUI to account for changes in ScreenPixelsPerInch by setting the Position property in character units (set via Units property).uicontrol('String','Moose','Units','characters','Position',[ 1 1 15 1.5 ],'FontSize',18);If clipped text persists for some labels, append a space to the end of the labels to force correct spacing.

Summary: Patch object with transparency does not display after changing axes limits
Description: Setting up a patch object with a partially transparent face and the axes limits to an area smaller than the patch itself causes the face color of the patch to go away. This has been reported for the following OpenGL hardware configuration: Version = 3.0.0 NVIDIA 180.29 Vendor = NVIDIA Corporation Renderer = Quadro FX 570/PCI/SSE2 MaxTextureSize = 8192 Visual = 0x26 (TrueColor, depth 24, RGB mask 0xff0000 0xff00 0x00ff) Software = false # of Extensions = 156
Workaround: Before R2014b, set the Renderer property of the figure to 'painters' or 'zbuffer'.set(gcf,'Renderer','painters')?

Summary: Closing an old figure and opening a new figure from a KeyPressFcn callback can cause MATLAB to unexpectedly exit
Description: Recursively closing and reopening a figure from its KeyPressFcn  or WindowKeyPressFcn  using a script or function can cause MATLAB to exit unexpectedly when the callback function executes. For example, consider a script named TryToCrash.m  that contains the following code: figure('KeyPressFcn','close(gcbf),TryToCrash');  If you execute this script, click in the figure window, and press any key repeatedly, MATLAB will unexpectedly exit.

Summary: YLabel with TeX looks incorrect if the font is Consolas size 7 or 8.
Description: An exponent in the label on the y-axis looks wrong if the font is Consolas  and the size is 7 or 8. The exponent is not offset correctly. Example: plot(1:10)ylabel('xxxx^2', 'fontname', 'Consolas', 'fontsize', 8)xlabel('xxxx^2', 'fontname', 'Consolas', 'fontsize', 8)  The x label looks correct, while the y label does not. ?
Workaround: Try a different font, or size 6 or 9.?

Summary: OpenGL crashes with certain graphic cards
Description: Using plotting functions that use OpenGL might result in the following crash: Stack Trace:   [0] 0x00000000000001c1(1, 0x407cb020, 1, 0x2abef6bb22f8 "Could not access OpenGL library")   [1] glren.so:OpenGLBootstrapOpengl()(0, 0x2aaac4c2bd90, 0x407cabb0, 0x407cacf0 ") + 73 bytes   [2] glren.so:OpenGLGetData(0x2aaac4c2bd90, 600, 600, 0x2aaacce3d200) + 150 bytes   [3] libmwhg.so:OpenGLSetupEnumstrOnce(bool)(0xafa28200000002, 0, 0, 0) + 268 bytes ...  One cause for the above crash is an OpenGL  incompatibility between MATLAB  and the graphics hardware. This typically happens when the graphics driver is not up to date.
Workaround: Before R2014b, set the Renderer property of the figure to 'painters' or 'zbuffer':set(gcf,'Renderer','painters')?

Summary: Saving and loading MAT-files containing MATLAB figures and objects returned by the linkprop command causes segmentation violations to occur.
Description: When saving and loading MAT-files containing MATLAB figures and objects returned by the linkprop  command as in the following code, MATLAB will crash with a segmentation violation. figure; h1 = axes; h2 = axes;  trashvar1 = linkprop([h1 h2],{'Units', 'ActivePositionProperty'}); % Allow linked resizing 'A' pause save('temp.mat'); %OK runErrorRate = [.1 .5]; runTotal = [10 10]; x = [1 2]; [tmp confInt] = berconfint(round(runErrorRate.*runTotal), runTotal, 0.90); e = runErrorRate(:) - confInt(:, 1); confInt90 = errorbar(h1, x, runErrorRate(:), e); 'B' pause save('temp.mat'); pause close all;clear all; load temp.mat close all
Workaround: To work around this issue, be sure to clear any variables containing objects returned by the linkprop command prior to saving your work space.

Summary: In R2009a, a bug was introduced which could cause an exception to be thrown in the handle graphics hit testing code.
Description: This exception could be encountered in commands like ginput, gtext, or zoom. The stack trace will look like: Stack Trace: [0] hg.dll:void __cdecl gs_data3matrix_to_pixel(double *,double *,double *,struct GObject_tag *,struct Real_point3 *,int)(0x52780020, 0x573d0020, 0, 0x1cf5a990) + 633 bytes [1] hg.dll:public: virtual void __thiscall HgCurveDescriptor::transformToPixels(struct Real_point3 *,int)(0, 0x00989680, 0, 0x1cf5ad20) + 45 bytes [2] libmwgui.dll:bool __cdecl gui_objects::isCurveInRectangle(class gui_objects::CurveDescriptor &,class gui_objects::Rectangle2i const &)(528, 0x00c2d0d0, 0x00c2d2c8, 525) + 729 bytes [3] hg.dll:bool __cdecl CurveInRect(struct GObject_tag *,struct mwrect *)(0x1cf5ad20, 0x00c2d128, 0x00c2d154 "¡è??", 0x7883e09b) + 78 bytes [4] hg.dll:_GO_ObjectInRect(0x1cf5ad20, 0x00c2d128, 0x1ce86388, 0x022a94e8) + 22 bytes [5] hg.dll:struct GObject_tag * __cdecl pt_in_object(struct mwpoint *,struct GObject_tag *,class Vector *)(0x00c2d2c8, 0x1cf5ad20, 0, 0x1ce86388) + 699 bytes

Summary: contour offsets output level when input level exactly equals the minimum data value.
Description: The lowest contour level in the resulting plot is labeled 1e-7: z=[zeros(3) ones(3)];lim=[0 1];[C,h]=contour(z,lim);clab=clabel(C,h);
Workaround: If this behavior is undesired, check whether any level is exactly equal to the data minimum before calling contour, and eliminate that level from the input level list.

Summary: Resizing or moving the figure clears the last movie frame
Description: Once a movie has finished playing, resizing or moving the figure clears the last frame. This also happens if you minimized and then maximized the figure.
Workaround: To work around this issue, display the last frame of the movie as an image using the image function.For example:surf(peaks); movie_frames = getframe(); fig = figure; % Reset figure size: This is optional[h, w, p] = size(movie_frames(1).cdata);set(fig,'Position',[150 150 w h]);axis off; % Play movie twice at bottom left of figuremovie(fig,movie_frames,2); % Save last frame on the figure as image  image(movie_frames(end).cdata); % Resize axis to match size of the movie frame % This ensures that the image matches the size of the % movie frame. set(gca,'Position',[0 0 1 1]); axis off;

Summary: Text with a negative Rotation value isn't drawn on Linux.?
Description: When you use the set  command to specify a negative value for the Text Rotation  property, the negative angle is automatically converted to a positive one. For example, -90 is converted to 270. Using the Property Editor , you can specify a negative value Rotation  property on a Text  object. This avoids the conversion to a positive value, but the text object is not drawn on Linux. ? ?
Workaround: Set the Rotation value to a positive angle instead of negative. For example, instead of -90, put in 270. Instead of -15, use 345.?

Summary: When positioning text objects in plot edit mode using the keyboard, the text can move in unexpected directions
Description: When you move text objects selected in plot edit mode with an arrow key, the text does not always move in the direction of the arrow.
Workaround: You can work around this issue by pressing and holding down an arrow key; the text object might initially move in the wrong direction, but the repeating movements go in the direction of the arrow.Alternatively, to make the arrow keys work correctly all the time, set the Units property of the text object to anything other than Data before attempting to reposition it. Use Normalized units to enable proportional resize behavior, which Data units also enables.The following code illustrates the issue and the workaround.figure; axes;title('Move me with arrow keys');plotedit on% Select the title and reposition with arrow keys; it moves incorrectlyht = get(gca,'Title');get(ht,'Units');  %Data units is defaultset(ht,'Units','Normalized');  %This corrects the problem% Reposition title with arrow keys; it now moves as it should.?

Summary: The waitforbuttonpress function suppresses a figure's WindowButtonDownFcn callback
Description: A figure's WindowButtonDownFcn callback does not execute when the  waitforbuttonpress function is called. The following functions demonstrate the issue:function buttonbugclose all; clcset(gcf, 'WindowButtonDownFcn', @buttonDown);title('Hit any key to exit');switch waitforbuttonpress    case 0        disp('Mouse Button down detected')    case 1        disp('Key button down detected')end function buttonDown(axisHandle, event)disp('WindowButtonDownFcn callback executed')
